title: I/O Redirection
summary: Bash provides operators to redirect the standard streams — stdin (0), stdout (1), and stderr (2) — to files or other commands. This lets you control where output goes and where input comes from without changing the program itself.
keyPoints: < passes a file's contents as stdin
| pipes stdout of one command into stdin of another

exercises.prompt: Which operator appends stdout to a file without overwriting it?
exercises.explanation: > overwrites. >> opens the file in append mode so existing content is preserved.

exercises.prompt: Complete the command to redirect stderr to a file called errors.log.
exercises.explanation: File descriptor 2 is stderr. 2> redirects it just like > redirects stdout.

exercises.prompt: Write a one-liner that runs 'ls /fakepath', discards any error output, and appends the stdout to a file called out.txt.
exercises.hint: Use 2>/dev/null to discard stderr and >> to append stdout.
